text: Lecture	  8	  –	  Shoulder	  Girdle	  and	  Joint	   Shoulder	  Joint	   • Scapulohumeral	  Joint	  (Shoulder	  Joint)	   o Synovial	   o A	  ball	  and	  socket	  joint	  –	  moves	  in	  all	  axes	   • Shoulder	  girdle	   o Anchors	  upper	  limb	  to	  axial	  skeleton	   o Scapula	  and	  Clavicle	   o Girdle	  moves	  at:	    Sternoclavicular	  joint	  (A)	    Acromioclavicular	  joint	  (B)	  –	  little	  movement	  	   o Functionally	  act	  together.	   • Scapulohumeral	  Joint	  and	  Shoulder	  girdle	  function	  as	  a	  single	  unit.	   	   Scapulohumeral	  Joint	   • Glenoid	  Fossa	  on	  scapula	   o A	  very	  shallow	  socket	   o Deepened	  slightly	  by	  glenoid	  labrum	  (White	  Fibrocartilage)	    Attached	  to	  margin	  of	  glenoid	  fossa	    Also	  able	  to	  change	  shape	  slightly	  to	  accommodate	  different	  parts	  of	   the	  humeral	  head	  during	  movement	   • Humerus	   o Head	  is	  half	  sphere.	   o Doesn’t	  fit	  entirely	  into	  the	  glenoid	  fossa	  	  some	  of	  it	  is	  non	  articular.	   	   Capsule	  	   • Attachment	  	   o follows	  border	  of	  articular	  head	  of	  humerus	  superiorly	  	   o reaches	  slightly	  more	  inferior	  (1cm)	  to	  articular	  surface	  on	  humeral	  head	   caudally	  	   o blends	  with	  the	  labrum	   • Glenohumeral	  ligaments	  (upper,	  middle	  and	  lower)	  –	  intrinsic	  anterior	  thickening	  of	  capsule	   o Can	  not	  be	  seen	  exteriorly	   • In	  adducted	  arm,	  capsule	  is	  fully	  stretched	  superiorly	  and	  bunched	  together	  inferiorly.	   • In	  abducted	  arm,	  capsule	  is	  fully	  stretched	  inferiorly	  and	  bunched	  together	  superiorly.	  	   • It’s	  weakest	  at	  its	  inferior	  border.	  WHY	   	   Coracoacromial	  arch	  and	  ligament	  	   • Form	  a	  roof	  over	  the	  joint	  =	  prevention	  from	  upward	  displacement	  	   • Protects	  the	  joint	  from	  over	  abduction	   	   Rotator	  Cuff	   • Rotator	  cuff	  muscles	  function	  to	  keep	  humerus	  head	  from	  slipping	  out	  of	  glenoid	  fossa.	   o Dynamically	  adjust	  their	  tension	  depending	  on	  location	  of	  humerus.	   • Greater	  Tubercle	  (Posteriorly/laterally)	   o Supraspinatus	  (1)	  –	  doesn’t	  rotate	  the	  joint.	   o Infraspinatus	  (2)	  –	  Lateral	  rotator	   o Teres	  Minor	  (3)	  	  –	  Lateral	  rotator	   • Lesser	  Tubercle	  (anteriorly)	   o Subscapularis	  muscle	  (4)	  –	  medial	  rotator	  (it	  has	  a	  minor	  role	  compared	  to	  Pect	   Major	  and	  Lat	  Dorsi)	   • Most	  important	  function	  is	  stability	  of	  the	  shoulder	  joint	  	   	   	   	   7 Rotator cuff Muscles close to the scapula. (Named accordingly) Pass close to the capsule, partly blend with it. Attachments to humerus correspond. Supraspinatus (1) Infraspinatus (2) Teres minor (3) Subscapularis.(4) Three (of four) are ‘rotators’. ©Avinash Bharadwaj 2011 1 2 3 4 1 2 3 4 5 Capsule Anterior : Thickening Glenohumeral ligaments x3. Not seen from exterior. In the ‘dependent’ position : Capsule stretched at the top, Loose inferiorly In abduction : Head stretches inferior part. Potential weak areas. “Protection”…? ©Avinash Bharadwaj 2011 	   	   Joint	  Stability	   • Mobility	  vs	  Stability	   • Factors	   o Bone:	  If	  they	  fit	  well	  together,	  eg	  hip	  joint	  fits	  really	  well,	  shoulder	  doesn’t.	   o Capsule	  and	  Ligaments:	  Thicker	  vs	  thinner/weaker	  portions	   o Muscles:	  muscles	  to	  stabilise	  the Yes	   Subscapularis	   	   	   	   	   Yes	   	   	   Abduction/Adduction	   • Supraspinatus	  must	  do	  first	  10	  to	  20° then	  deltoid	  takes	  over.	   • Limited	  by	  bone	  structures,	  greater	  tuberosity	  and	  acromion	   • After	  90	  degrees	  shoulder	  girdle	  must	  rotate	   	   Rotation	   • Think	  of	  where	  muscle	  is	  inserted	  onto	  and	  where	  it	  has	  come	  from	   • Medial:	  	   o Pect	  Major,	  Lat	  Dorsi,	  Subscapularis,	  Teres	  Major	   • Lateral	   o Infraspinatus,	  teres	  minor	  	   	   Other	  Muscles	   • Biceps	  Brachii	   o Both	  heads	  attached	  to	  scapula	   o Supraglenoid	  tubercle	  (long	  head)	  and	  coracoid	  (short	  head)	   o Long	  head	  is	  intracapsular,	  thus	  can	  stabilise	  the	  joint	   • Triceps	  Brachii	   o Long	  head	  attaches	  to	  the	  scapula	  (infraglenoid	  tubercle)	    Useful	  as	  a	  stabiliser	   o 2	  Short	  heads	  are	  humeral.	   	   Bursae	   • Fluid	  filled	  sacs	  full	  of	  synovial	  fluid	  	   • Many	  in	  the	  joint	   • Reduces	  friction	  between	  various	  parts,	  eg	  ligaments.	   • Constant	  ones:	   o Subacromial/subdeltoid	  	  subject	  to	  damage.	    between	  acromion	  and	  supraspinatus,	  then	  between	  deltoid	  and	  head	   of	  humerus	   o Subscapular	    between	  subscapularis	  and	  head	  of	  humerus	   17 Bursae around shoulder joint Numerous! Constant ones : Subacromial ‘subdeltoid’ Subject to injury Subscapular May be continuous with synovial membrane Other bursae Between various muscles and tendons ©Avinash Bharadwaj 2011 11 P. Major and L. dorsi : Two main players! Pectoralis major : Flexion and adduction Latissimus dorsi Extension and adduction Together : Adduction. Other flexors : any muscle/s anterior to the shoulder E.g. subscapularis, part of deltoid Other extensors : any muscle posterior E.g. infraspinatus, part of deltoid P. major L. dorsi ©Avinash Bharadwaj 2011  Continuous	  with	  synovial	  membrane	  of	  glenohumeral	  joint	   	   Girdle	  Movements	   • Rotation	  of	  the	  scapula	   o Clavicle	  moves	  with	  it	   o Moves	  glenoid	  fossa	  to	  face	  down	  or	  up	   	   Muscles	  	   • Trapezius	   o Upper	  and	  lower	  fibres	  contracting	  together	  rotate	  glenoid	   upwards	   • Serratus	  Anterior	   o Upper	  and	  lower	  fibres	  contracting	  together	  rotate	  glenoid	  downwards	  	   o also	  –	  pulls	  medial	  border	  of	  scapula	  forwards	  =	  protraction	   	   Clinical	  (often	  due	  to	  increased	  mobility	  and	  decreased	  stability):	  	   • Loose	  capsule	  =	  prone	  to	  injury	  	   • Dislocation	  (usually	  inferiorly)	  –	  occurs	  due	  to	  a	  relative	  weakness	  inferiorly	   o Superiorly	  lies	  the	  coracoacromial	  arch	  (coracoid	  process,	  coracoacromial	  ligaments	  and	   acromion	  process)	  as	  well	  as	  the	  tendons	  on	  the	  supraspinatus,	  biceps	  brachii	  and	  deltoid.	  In	   addition,	  the	  glenohumeral	  (sup,	  middle	  and	  inf)	  and	  the	  coracohumeral	  ligaments	  lie	   anterosuperiorly.	  	   • Wear	  and	  tear	  	   o rotator	  cuff	  and	  acromial	  bursa	   o greater	  tuberosity	  ‘impingement’	   o supraspinatous	  tendon	  	   • Shoulder	  pain	   	   Sternoclavicular	  joint	  	   • Saddle	  synovial	  joint	   • The	  only	  articulation	  btw	  the	  upper	  limb	  and	  axial	  skeleton	   • Clavicle	  +	  manubrium	  +	  1st	  costal	  cartilage	   • Anterior	  and	  posterior	  Sternoclavicular	  ligaments	  	   • Interclavicular	  ligament	  (superiorly	  on	  capsule)	   • Costoclavicular	  ligament	   	   Acromioclavicular	  joint	  	   • Plane	  synovial	  joint	  	   • 2-­‐3cm	  from	  the	  point	  of	  the	  shoulder	   • Acromioclavicular,	  Coracoclavicular	  (trapezoid	  (lat)	  and	  conoid	  (med))	  ligament	   	   	   19 Girdle Movements Trapezius General direction of pull : upwards / medial (shrugging / ‘bracing’) Upper + Lower parts rotate glenoid upwards. Serratus anterior : pulls medial border forward ‘protraction’. However, upper and lower fibres acting alone rotate the scapula. ©Avinash Bharadwaj 2011 19 Girdle Movements Trapezius General direction of pull : upwards / medial (shrugging / ‘bracing’) Upper + Lower parts rotate glenoid upwards. Serratus anterior : pulls medial border forward ‘protraction’. However, upper and lower fibres acting alone rotate the scapula. ©Avinash Bharadwaj 2011
